file_name: PlayboyEnterprisesInc_20090220_10-QA_EX-10.2_4091580_EX-10.2_Content License Agreement_ Marketing Agreement_ Sales-Purchase Agreement1.txt

perturbation.original_text: Client agrees to register and maintain the applicable domain name extensions in each such market, including .ca, .au, .com, and .uk for the Websites; provided that such domain names are available. Client acknowledges that the functionality for local billing will not be deployed by March 1, 2008, but will be deployed for the UK and Australia not later than ninety (90) days following launch of the Websites.
perturbation.changed_text: Client agrees to use commercially reasonable efforts to register and maintain domain names for the Websites. Client acknowledges that the functionality for local billing will not be deployed by March 1, 2008.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific domain name extensions (.ca, .au, .com, .uk) that Client agrees to register and maintain, this creates an ambiguity as to Client's obligations. The clause 'Client agrees to use commercially reasonable efforts to register and maintain domain names for the Websites' is vague. This omission also introduces a contradiction because later in Section 3.1, the contract indicates, ‘For any market that is included in the initial Website Territory or is added later, EFS agrees to (a) actively market, including, but not limited to, via SEO (optimizing the Websites and creating custom pages to maximize search engine optimization in local markets), search engine marketing and affiliate marketing, (b) provide local billing and customized Websites for local audiences, vis a vis language and targeted merchandising, and (c) provide competitive and timely fulfillment.’ EFS' obligation to actively market to local audiences is weakened if the Client's responsibility to secure the domain is not explicitly stated, this is an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: For any market that is included in the initial Website Territory or is added later, EFS agrees to (a) actively market, including, but not limited to, via SEO (optimizing the Websites and creating custom pages to maximize search engine optimization in local markets), search engine marketing and affiliate marketing, (b) provide local billing and customized Websites for local audiences, vis a vis language and targeted merchandising, and (c) provide competitive and timely fulfillment.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states the domain name extensions Client agrees to register and maintain. The changed text weakens this obligation to 'use commercially reasonable efforts to register and maintain domain names.' This creates an ambiguity because the Client's obligations are no longer explicit. The explanation correctly identifies that this omission creates a contradiction with EFS's obligation to actively market to local audiences as per the Contradicted Text, because EFS's marketing efforts are weakened if the Client's domain name registration responsibility isn't explicitly stated. This constitutes a direct, impactful contradiction, affecting the clarity and enforceability of each party's responsibilities.

perturbation.original_text: EFS shall, at its own expense, submit to Client or its designee for written approval, samples of any EFS Produced Merchandise and the Materials at each stage of development thereof, which shall include, but not be limited to: (i) an initial sketch or photograph prior to any manufacture thereof; (ii) a sample prototype or equivalent acceptable to Client; and (iii) two final production-quality samples of that which will be mass produced or manufactured. EFS must obtain Client's written approval of each stage of development before proceeding to the next stage, and in no event shall EFS commence or permit the mass manufacture, advertising, promotion, sale or distribution of any EFS Produced Merchandise or Materials unless and until EFS has received Client's written approval of the samples provided pursuant to (iii) of this Section 1.
perturbation.changed_text: EFS shall submit to Client or its designee for approval, samples of any EFS Produced Merchandise and the Materials at each stage of development thereof. EFS must obtain Client's approval of each stage of development before proceeding to the next stage.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific requirements of submitting (i) an initial sketch or photograph, (ii) a sample prototype, and (iii) two final production-quality samples and the phrase 'at its own expense', the approval process becomes vague. The requirement for the stages to be submitted remains, but what constitutes each stage is undefined. This omission creates an inconsistency in Section 2.6(a), which states that EFS Produced Merchandise must adhere to Client's guidelines which can't be applied if there is no specific process for approval, hence it is an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Subject to provisions in existing agreements as disclosed herein on Exhibit 7, which shall be updated from time-to-time, Client's prior written consent and the Licensees' right of first refusal as set forth below, EFS shall be permitted to design and manufacture, or arrange for third parties (which have been approved by Client in advance in writing, said approval not to be unreasonably withheld) to manufacture on its behalf, products branded with the Playboy Marks to be sold on the Websites and/or through the Catalogs ("EFS Produced Merchandise"). EFS agrees that it shall approach Licensees regarding any planned EFS Produced Merchandise and give such Licensees a seven (7) day right of first refusal with respect to the design and manufacture thereof, whereby EFS may set forth commercially reasonable requirements with respect to pricing, delivery and product specifications. If such Licensee is unwilling or unable to comply with EFS' request, EFS shall be free to proceed with the manufacturing of EFS Produced Merchandise as set forth in this Section 2.6. *****
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the specific approval process for 'EFS Produced Merchandise' and 'Materials,' making the 'Client's prior written consent' requirement in the contradicted text (Section 2.6) difficult to enforce. Without defined stages of development and specific sample requirements, it's unclear how Client can reasonably grant or withhold consent for the design and manufacture of products branded with the Playboy Marks. This creates a contradiction because the original Section 1 established a defined approval process, which is now missing, leading to ambiguity and potential disputes over compliance with Client's guidelines. This aligns with 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: EFS shall be responsible for maintaining the Advertisements on the Websites. EFS shall ensure that the Advertisements do not violate any Law or right of any third party or otherwise contain content reasonably likely to diminish the value of Client's brand or detract from the goodwill of the Client. Upon written request of Client, EFS shall discontinue or modify any Advertisement that in the reasonable opinion of Client is not appropriate for the Client brand or is competitive with Client business.
perturbation.changed_text: EFS shall be responsible for maintaining the Advertisements on the Websites. EFS shall ensure that the Advertisements do not violate any Law or right of any third party. Upon written request of Client, EFS shall discontinue or modify any Advertisement.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting 'or otherwise contain content reasonably likely to diminish the value of Client's brand or detract from the goodwill of the Client' and 'in the reasonable opinion of Client is not appropriate for the Client brand or is competitive with Client business', the criteria for Client to request a change to advertisement is now less specific. The text provides no standards to the types of advertisement EFS should consider. This conflicts with Section 4.4 which grants EFS the right to 'sell and place on the Websites promotional banner advertisements (the frequency, placement and volume of which to be mutually agreed upon by the parties) that advertise and market third party products and services that do not compete with Client's products or services and comply with Client's advertising guidelines'. If EFS approves promotional banner advertisements on the websites, this is a contradiction with the clause that the advertisements must be approved by the client, it is now less clear that advertisements have a standard that is considered valid by Client.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Client grants EFS the right to: (a) sell and place on the Websites promotional banner advertisements (the frequency, placement and volume of which to be mutually agreed upon by the parties) that advertise and market third party products and services that do not compete with Client's products or services and comply with Client's advertising guidelines ("Advertisements"), provided that such advertising efforts are mutual (i.e., equivalent promotion of Client and its affiliates on third party websites) and are executed in a manner that minimizes adverse impact on sales; and (b) send emails containing Advertisements to customers who specifically opt in to receive email notifications from EFS and Client.
perturbation.justification: The omission in the changed text removes the Client's explicit control over advertisement content that diminishes their brand or competes with their business, which directly conflicts with the contradicted text granting EFS the right to sell advertisements that comply with the Client's advertising guidelines and do not compete with the Client's products. The change creates uncertainty about whether EFS can approve advertisements without Client oversight, thereby contradicting the original intent and creating a loophole that would allow EFS to approve certain banners even if the client finds them harmful.
